x x x x x x x x
You can distinguish CLI execution without extra variables.
Flask provides a built-in way to detect the context.
The `click` library powers the CLI commands in Flask.
You can inspect the current `click` context object.
This context reveals if the command is running via CLI.
A server request will not have this specific context.
You can check the `ctx.invoked_subcommand` attribute inside.
This allows logic to branch based on execution mode.
Your `create_app` function can accept a custom flag parameter.
The CLI command can pass this flag directly to the function.
You do not need to rely on environment variables for this.
Define a custom option in your CLI command decorator.
This option can set a boolean flag for the app factory.
The `create_app` function reads this flag to configure itself.
You might need to modify how the app factory is registered.
Flask allows passing arguments to the app factory via CLI.
This is done by using the `with_appcontext` pattern carefully.
You can override the default behavior in your specific command.
The command function receives the context as an argument.
From there you can call `create_app` with your specific flag.
This flag tells the factory to skip server-specific setup.
It ensures the CLI runs with minimal overhead and correct config.
x x x x x x x x
This approach keeps your code clean and modular.
No extra environment variables are required for this logic.
The distinction happens automatically at runtime.
Your application becomes more flexible and easier to manage.
You have now solved the problem without external inputs.
